topic: Hello — who I am and why I'm here
text:
G'day, I'm Edward. I've spent years in disability support, and I started this page to share the practical stuff that makes a support worker's week lighter — the notes, the price guide, getting paid, the admin that quietly steals your evenings.

No pitch here, just things I've learned the hard way and want to pass on. If you're a sole trader or running a small provider, tell me below: what's the one task you wish would just do itself? I read and reply to everything. 👋

topic: The 3 admin tasks that quietly eat your week
text:
Three culprits I see again and again:

1. Progress notes after every shift — do them in the car before you drive off, while it's fresh. Five minutes now beats an hour on Sunday.
2. Chasing invoices/claims — keep a one-line tracker: date, participant, amount, paid Y/N. You can't chase what you can't see.
3. Re-finding info — one folder per participant, same structure every time. Future-you will thank you.

What's the fourth one I missed? 👇

topic: What's the most confusing part of the NDIS price guide for you?
text:
Honest question for the support workers here: what's the most confusing part of the NDIS price guide for you?

Line items, travel, cancellations, the wording that changes every update — no wrong answers. I'll do my best to point to a clear answer for each one in the comments.

topic: A clean progress-note template that survives an audit
text:
A note that holds up under audit usually has five things: date & time, who was there, what the goal was, what actually happened, and the participant's response. Plain language, factual, no opinions dressed as facts.

Quick test: if someone read your note in two years with zero context, would they understand what happened and why? If yes, you're sorted.

Save this one for your next shift. What does your note routine look like?

topic: Getting paid faster: invoicing + claims checklist
text:
A getting-paid checklist for sole traders:

☐ Service agreement signed before you start
☐ Notes done same day (your invoice evidence)
☐ Invoice within 48 hours of the shift
☐ Clear line items matching the plan
☐ A follow-up date in your calendar if unpaid in 14 days

Cash flow isn't about working more — it's about closing the loop faster. What's your average wait to get paid?

topic: The admin night that made me start building something
text:
Real talk. A while back I finished a run of shifts, sat down at 9pm, and spent another two hours on notes and invoices. Care all day, paperwork all night. I remember thinking: the record-keeping shouldn't cost this much of the actual caring.

That night is why I started building something. More on that soon — but first, am I the only one? How late does your admin keep you up?

topic: Solo support work can be isolating — how do you stay connected?
text:
Support work can be lonely — you're often the only one in the room, and there's no team kitchen to debrief in.

So how do you stay connected? A group chat, a mentor, a Facebook community like this one? Share what works — someone reading this needs to hear it. 🙂

topic: What I've been building — want to be first to try it?
text:
Okay — full honesty, here's what I've been building. It's called Caira. Care is the work. Caira handles the record.

The idea: take the documentation off a support worker's plate — notes, records and reporting — so the hours go back to care instead of paperwork. It's not out yet; I'm getting it ready properly and want a few real providers to help shape it before launch.

If you'd like to be first in line and have a say in how it works, comment or DM me. No cost, no pressure — just building this with the people who'll use it. 🙏

topic: Travel and the bits people forget to claim
text:
A few things support workers leave on the table:

• Provider travel between participants, where the plan and current rules allow
• Non-labour travel costs (the km) under the current pricing arrangements
• Short-notice cancellations — know the rule before you write the shift off

The pricing arrangements change, so always check the current ones rather than what you remember from last year. And log your travel as you go, not from memory on Sunday. What's something you only found out you could claim months in?

topic: Capturing notes during a shift, not at 9pm
text:
The tip that gave me my evenings back: don't wait till you're home. Capture the note while it's fresh — a quick voice memo in the car, a line in your notes app, even paper. Three bullets in the moment (what was the goal, what happened, how they responded) beat a blank page at 9pm every time.

Whatever tool you use, the trick is the same: capture now, polish later. How do you capture notes during a shift?

topic: What eats your week that has nothing to do with actual support?
text:
Honest one: what eats your week that has nothing to do with the actual support work?

Notes, invoicing, chasing payments, finding documents, travel admin? Name your biggest time-sink below — I'm curious what comes up most, and there might be a fix worth sharing.

topic: Build-in-public: the feedback that changed what I'm building
text:
Building in public: a support worker told me last week that the scary part of notes isn't writing them — it's wondering whether they'd hold up if anyone ever asked.

That stuck with me. Spent the weekend reworking how Caira (the tool I'm building) structures a note so the important bones are there by default. Still pre-launch — shaping it with the people who'll actually use it.

So: what would make you genuinely confident in your notes?

topic: Person-first language, the short version
text:
Person-first language, the short version:

• 'a person with disability', not 'a disabled person' (in most AU contexts)
• describe the support, not the deficit
• 'uses a wheelchair', not 'wheelchair-bound'
• and ask the person how they describe themselves — they're the authority

It shows up in your notes too: factual, respectful, about the person not the label. I put together a one-page cheat-sheet on this — happy to share it, just say the word. What language did you have to unlearn when you started?

topic: Incident notes without the panic
text:
When something goes wrong, a good incident note is calm and factual: what happened, when, who was involved, what you did, and who you told.

Stick to what you observed — don't write a guess at the cause as if it's a fact. Write it as soon as it's safe to, while the detail's sharp. And know your provider's reporting timeframes before you ever need them.

How does your team handle incident reporting?

topic: Best advice you got when you started as a support worker?
text:
What's the best piece of advice you got when you started as a support worker?

Drop it below — there are new workers reading this who'll genuinely thank you for it. I'll start in the comments.

topic: Caira update — early feedback is making it sharper
text:
Quick Caira update (the tool I'm building for support-worker notes). The first handful of providers giving feedback have been brutal in the best way — and it's made it sharper. Care is the work. Caira handles the record.

Still pre-launch. I'm taking on a few more people who want to shape it and be first in line. If that's you, comment or DM — no cost, just honest feedback wanted. 🙏

topic: Setting your rates as a sole trader
text:
Setting your rate as a sole trader isn't just "what's the cap". Two things to factor in:

• The current NDIS pricing arrangements set the ceiling — always work from the current ones, not last year's.
• Your real costs sit under that: travel, admin time, insurance, super, the gaps between shifts. The headline rate isn't what lands in your pocket.

Work out your actual hourly cost first, then price with eyes open. (This is general info, not financial advice — worth a chat with an accountant.) How did you land on your rate?

topic: Professional boundaries with participants and families
text:
Good boundaries protect the relationship as much as they protect you.

A few that help:
• Set hours for messages, and let the after-hours ones wait till you're working.
• Be warm but clear about what's in scope for the shift.
• Have a simple line ready for gifts or favours that don't sit right.

None of it means being cold — it means being consistent, so everyone knows where they stand. How do you handle the after-hours message?

topic: How do you reset after a shift that went sideways?
text:
Some shifts just go sideways — and then you're expected to show up fresh for the next one.

How do you reset? A walk, a debrief with someone who gets it, a hard stop before home? Share what actually works for you — someone reading this is having one of those days right now.

topic: Build-in-public: first beta workers are in
text:
Building in public: the first support workers are now using Caira day to day, and it's the best kind of nerve-wracking.

The surprise so far? It's not the fancy stuff people want — it's the boring reliability of capturing a note fast and trusting it's all there. Good lesson. Still pre-launch, still shaping it with them.

For those of you who've tried tools before: what made you stop using one?

topic: Tax and GST basics for sole-trader support workers
text:
Tax basics that trip up new sole traders:

• You need an ABN to invoice.
• GST generally kicks in once you turn over $75k — under that, you usually don't charge it (check your situation).
• Keep every receipt — car, phone, training, gear.
• Set aside a slice of every payment for tax now, so June isn't a shock.

Big disclaimer: I'm a support worker, not an accountant — this is general info, get advice for your situation. What's your system for setting tax aside?

topic: Cutting last-minute cancellations
text:
Last-minute cancellations hurt when you're solo. A few things that help:

• A clear cancellation policy in your service agreement, agreed up front.
• A friendly reminder the day before.
• Knowing the current short-notice cancellation rules, so you claim correctly when one happens.

It's not about being rigid — it's about everyone knowing the deal in advance. What's your no-show rate like, and how do you handle it?

topic: What got you into support work?
text:
Genuine question: what got you into support work?

A person, a moment, a job that turned into a calling, or just fell into it and stayed? I love these stories — share yours below. I'll go first in the comments.

topic: Caira pre-launch: a few early spots left
text:
Caira's coming along (the notes tool I'm building for support workers). I'm keeping the early group small so I can actually listen to everyone in it — and there are a few spots left.

Care is the work. Caira handles the record.

If you'd like to help shape it and be first in line when it launches, comment or DM. No cost, no pressure — just honest feedback wanted. 🙏

topic: Handover notes when more than one worker is on
text:
When a participant has more than one worker, a good handover note saves everyone guessing.

Keep it short and useful: what happened this shift, anything that changed, anything the next worker needs to watch for or follow up. Facts, not vibes — and always written for the participant's benefit, not as a vent.

The test: could the next worker walk in and pick up cleanly? How does your team do handovers?

topic: Spotting burnout before it spots you
text:
Support work asks a lot of you, and burnout creeps in quietly. Some early signs: dreading shifts you used to enjoy, short fuse, notes feeling like a mountain, switching off from people you care about.

Small protective habits help — real breaks between shifts, a hard stop, someone to talk to. And it's okay to ask for help; it's a strength, not a failing.

Look after the person doing the caring. What helps you keep your tank from hitting empty?

topic: Share a small win from this week
text:
Let's do a good-news thread. Share one small win from your week — a breakthrough, a laugh, a goal ticked off, a participant moment that stuck with you.

The little ones count. Drop yours below. 🙂

topic: Build-in-public: what building solo has taught me
text:
Building Caira solo, around actual shifts, has taught me one thing hard: do less, but do it properly.

Every time I've been tempted to add something clever, the early users have pulled me back to "just make the basic thing fast and reliable". They're right. It's the same lesson the job teaches — show up, do the fundamentals well, every time.

Still pre-launch, still listening. What's one tool that won you over by being simple, not flashy?

topic: Goal-focused notes: linking what you do to a participant's goals
text:
A note becomes far more useful when it connects to the participant's goals.

Instead of just "went to the shops", try: which goal this supported (e.g. building independence with money or community access), what you actually did, and how they went. Same shift — but now the note shows progress, not just activity.

It helps the participant, the plan review, and you. Do you map your notes to goals already, or is it on the to-do list?

topic: Keeping your checks and screening current
text:
Boring but it'll bite you if it lapses — a quick credentials check:

• NDIS Worker Screening Check (know your expiry)
• Working With Children Check, where your work needs it
• First aid / CPR currency
• Any provider-specific training renewals

Pop the expiry dates in your calendar now with a reminder a month out. Check the official sources for your exact obligations. What's the one that always sneaks up on you?

topic: If your admin could vanish overnight, what goes first?
text:
Fun one: if a fairy could make one piece of your admin vanish overnight, what's the first to go?

Notes? Invoicing? Chasing payments? Timesheets? Tell me what you'd banish forever. 🧚

topic: Caira — what the first users are saying
text:
Two months of building Caira in the open with support workers, and a clear theme has emerged from the early group: the win isn't doing notes "better", it's not carrying them home in your head all evening.

That's the whole point. Care is the work. Caira handles the record.

Still pre-launch — if you'd like to be first in line and have a say, comment or DM. No cost, just honest feedback. 🙏

topic: Dignity and privacy in your notes — what NOT to include
text:
A quick one on what to leave OUT of a note.

Record what's relevant to the support and the participant's goals. Leave out: gossip, your personal opinions about family, unrelated medical detail you've overheard, anything that reads like a judgement.

Write every note as if the participant might read it — because they have the right to. Respectful and factual is the standard. What's your rule of thumb for what stays out?

topic: Juggling a schedule across several participants
text:
Running between participants? A few things that keep it sane:

• Build buffer time in for travel AND notes — back-to-back with zero gap is how you end up doing notes at midnight.
• Colour-code your calendar by participant or area.
• Protect a slice of weekly admin time like it's a shift.
• Don't let optimism double-book you.

The goal is a week you can actually sustain. How do you lay yours out?

topic: How do you keep work and life separate as a sole trader?
text:
When you're a sole trader, work can bleed into everything. How do you draw the line?

Phone off after a certain hour, a separate work number, a ritual that ends the day? Share what helps you actually switch off — we could all use the ideas.

topic: Build-in-public: the feature I scrapped
text:
Building in public, and this one stung a bit: I built a feature into Caira I was sure people would love. The early group barely touched it. So I cut it.

Lesson I keep re-learning — what I think is clever matters less than what actually helps on a busy shift. Better to do the simple things brilliantly. Still pre-launch, still listening.

What's a feature in any app you wish they'd just remove?

topic: Working well with support coordinators and plan managers
text:
Support coordinators and plan managers can make your working life smoother — if you make theirs easier.

What helps them: clear, timely updates; notes that actually show progress against goals; flagging issues early, not at crisis point; tidy invoices. A coordinator who trusts you sends you more work.

Treat the relationship as a two-way street. What's made one of these relationships work well for you?

topic: Duty of care and emergencies on shift
text:
When something serious happens on shift: safety first, stay calm, follow your training and your provider's procedures, then document and report properly.

The time to learn the emergency plan is BEFORE you need it — know who to call, what to record, and the reporting timeframes for your setting. Keep key numbers somewhere you can reach one-handed.

(This is a prompt to be prepared, not a substitute for proper training.) How do you keep emergency info handy on shift?

topic: What's a myth about support work you wish people understood?
text:
What's a myth about support work you wish people outside it understood?

"It's just babysitting", "anyone can do it", "it must be so sad"? Set the record straight below — I reckon this thread could be a good one to share.

topic: Caira's getting close — pre-launch update
text:
Caira update (the notes tool I'm building for support workers): we're getting close. The early group has put it through real shifts and it's holding up.

Care is the work. Caira handles the record.

Still a few spots if you'd like to be first in line and have a say before launch — comment or DM. No cost, just honest feedback wanted. 🙏

topic: Record-keeping for tax time — what to keep and for how long
text:
Tax-time record-keeping, the simple version:

• Keep invoices issued, receipts for expenses, and a logbook for car use.
• A digital folder per financial year beats a shoebox every time.
• As a rule, keep records for several years — check the ATO's current requirements for your situation.

Set the system up once and future-you sails through June. (General info, not tax advice — your accountant is the boss here.) What's your record-keeping setup?

topic: Building rapport with a new participant in week one
text:
First week with a new participant? Go slow.

Listen more than you talk. Follow their lead and their preferences. Be reliable in the small things — on time, do what you said you would. Trust is built in tiny consistent moments, not grand gestures.

You're a guest in their routine and often their home. Earn it gently. What's helped you build trust with someone new?

topic: Best book, podcast or group for support workers?
text:
Let's build a little resource list together.

What's the best book, podcast, course or group that's made you better at this work — or just kept you sane? Drop your pick below and I'll pull the best ones into a follow-up post.

topic: Build-in-public: nearly there — what's left before launch
text:
Build-in-public, near-launch edition. What's done: fast in-the-moment capture, the note structure, the day-to-day reliability the early group leaned on hardest. What's left: polishing the rough edges they flagged and getting the boring-but-important things right.

Huge thanks to the support workers who've shaped Caira so far — it's genuinely theirs as much as mine. What's the one thing that would make you trust a new tool on day one?

topic: Understanding a participant's plan — the basics
text:
Knowing how a participant's plan is managed changes how you work:

• Self-managed — they (or a nominee) handle the money and can be more flexible on who they use.
• Plan-managed — a plan manager pays your invoices.
• Agency-managed — claimed through the NDIA portal; you generally need to be a registered provider.

It affects how you invoice and what's possible. Always check the current official guidance. Which type do you work with most?

topic: Cultural safety in support work
text:
Cultural safety isn't a box to tick — it's how you show up.

Ask, don't assume. Learn the person's preferences around food, family, faith, language and personal space. Work WITH someone's culture, not over it. And if you get something wrong, own it and adjust.

The person is always the expert on their own life. What's something a participant taught you about doing this respectfully?

topic: Three months in — thank you, and what do you want more of?
text:
Three months of this page — thank you. Genuinely. The comments have taught me as much as anything.

Quick ask: what do you want more of? Practical how-tos, NDIS explainers, the business side, community threads like this? Tell me where to point next. 🙏

topic: Caira — where to from here
text:
Where Caira's at: built in the open with support workers, shaped by real shifts, and almost ready for more of you.

Care is the work. Caira handles the record.

If you've been following along and want to be first in line, comment or DM and I'll keep you posted. No cost, no pressure — thanks for being part of it. 🙏
